label: Classical Jain cosmology

scope.school: Shared classical doctrine (common to Digambara and Śvētāmbara)
scope.period: Mahāvīra traditionally dated c. 599–527 BCE (scholarly datings vary); classical philosophical systematization in the early centuries CE
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.
Mahāvīra's traditional dates are used by the sources; much scholarship places him later (5th century BCE) — the period scoping needs review.
Sectarian differences (Digambara vs. Śvētāmbara) and the detailed loka cosmography with its ascending/descending time cycles are not covered by the fetched sources.
No claims are recorded for time or destiny as separate primitives beyond liberation. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: The universe is beginningless and uncreated: 'the universe is eternal, matter and souls being equally uncreated', and 'what is denied is a creator god above all' — Jainism admits 'higher beings (devas), which are mortal', but no creator.
Reality divides into sentient and non-sentient substance: 'Jainism is dualistic—that is, matter and souls are thought to be entirely different types of substance'.
claims.notes: The two-substance gloss is a simplification: classical Jain ontology enumerates several non-sentient (ajīva) categories beyond matter — space, time, motion, and rest — so the dualism framing should be refined at review.

claims.claim: Souls are many, immortal, and intrinsically capable of omniscience: 'all living things possess an immortal soul which has always and will always exist', and the perfected soul attains 'kevala, the state of omniscience'.

Karma is literally material: 'by their actions, souls accumulate karma, which is understood to be a kind of matter' — and this 'karma as a material force distinguishes the Jain view from other Indian views'.
The inhabited universe is bounded: 'a finite region of space... is the only region of space that can contain anything'.
The soul's entanglement with karmic matter is the root predicament: accumulated karma binds the naturally luminous soul to embodiment and rebirth, obscuring its intrinsic omniscience.

Liberation is achieved by shedding all karma through discipline: 'release is achieved by purging the soul of all karma, good and bad'; 'the cardinal rule of interaction with other jīvas is the rule of ahimsa', and 'it is up to each individual to attain salvation... by adhering to a strict spiritual and ethical code of behavior'.

Ordinary knowledge is perspectival: 'reality is multifaceted (anekanta, or “non-one-sided”), such that no one view can capture it in its entirety', so assertions carry qualification — 'each predication is preceded by a marker of uncertainty (syat)'.

distinguishing_claims: An eternal, uncreated universe with no creator god
Karma is a material substance that physically binds souls
Every soul is intrinsically omniscient and individually responsible for its liberation
Anekāntavāda: all ordinary claims about reality are perspective-qualified
common_confusions: Jain nontheism is not atheism about all higher beings — devas exist but are mortal and did not create the world.
Jain dualism multiplies souls (like Sāṃkhya) but makes karma material and liberation a physical purging, not mere discriminative insight.
Anekāntavāda is not relativism: reality is determinate but too many-sided for any single predication.

sources.title: Jain Philosophy (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Mark Owen Webb
sources.passage: The universe is eternal, matter and souls being equally uncreated. [...] What is denied is a creator god above all. [...] Jainism is dualistic—that is, matter and souls are thought to be entirely different types of substance. [...] By their actions, souls accumulate karma, which is understood to be a kind of matter. [...] Release is achieved by purging the soul of all karma, good and bad. [...] The cardinal rule of interaction with other jīvas is the rule of ahimsa. [...] Reality is multifaceted (anekanta, or 'non-one-sided'), such that no one view can capture it in its entirety. [...] Each predication is preceded by a marker of uncertainty (syat), which I have rendered here as 'perhaps.' [...] kevala, the state of omniscience of a perfected soul. [...] A finite region of space... is the only region of space that can contain anything.

sources.title: Jainism (World History Encyclopedia)
sources.author: Joshua J. Mark
sources.passage: Jainism is a nontheistic religion in that it does not advocate a belief in a creator god but in higher beings (devas), which are mortal. [...] all living things possess an immortal soul which has always and will always exist. [...] karma as a material force distinguishes the Jain view from other Indian views. [...] Vardhamana (better known as Mahavira, l. c. 599-527 BCE), but he is actually only the 24th tirthankara. [...] In Jainism, it is up to each individual to attain salvation... by adhering to a strict spiritual and ethical code of behavior. (Published 2020-09-21.)

review.notes: Drafted for D2-001 batch 5. Source URLs fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Review needed: no human domain review yet; refine the ajīva ontology and Mahāvīra dating, and add a primary text (Tattvārtha Sūtra) at review.
